policy_statement: Develop Climate Change Adaptation Plans and set up forecasting, warning and response systems, including climate prediction models for extreme phenomena and population warning.
mechanism_description: Developing climate change adaptation plans and setting up forecasting, warning, and response systems improves preparedness and response to extreme climate phenomena, reducing associated impacts on people and assets.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard → improved preparedness/early warning → ↓ exposure & vulnerability → ↓ climate impact damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Governments and agencies create adaptation plans, establish hazard forecasting and monitoring, define triggers and protocols, and implement public alerting and coordinated emergency response.  
(2) Mediating physical/social process: Better prediction and faster risk communication increase lead time; preparedness measures (evacuation, asset protection, service continuity) reduce the degree to which people and infrastructure are exposed to hazardous conditions and reduce sensitivity during events.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower mortality and morbidity, reduced infrastructure/economic losses, and faster recovery from climate-related extremes (heatwaves, floods, storms, wildfires, drought-related crises).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced loss of life, damage, and disruption from extreme weather and climate hazards through earlier warning and better-prepared response.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core chain is “climate hazard information + preparedness → reduced exposure/vulnerability → reduced impacts,” which is adaptation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While planning can incidentally influence mitigation (e.g., resilient infrastructure choices) or efficiency, the direct, largest effect across policies using this mechanism is impact reduction during hazards, not emissions reduction or resource-input reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an “adaptation plan” includes explicit mitigation actions, those would be separate mechanisms; the early warning/preparedness component still primarily functions as adaptation. Early warning for non-climate hazards (e.g., earthquakes) would fall outside this mechanism’s scope.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that develops adaptation plans and/or forecasting-warning-response systems aims to manage climate risk and reduce damages from extremes, so Adaptation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific adaptation subtypes (heat, flood, wildfire; community resilience vs. critical infrastructure continuity) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “response systems” include major infrastructure hardening or nature-based buffers, those additional elements may introduce separate mitigation/NBS/resource-efficiency mechanisms, but the early-warning/planning mechanism remains primarily Adaptation.
key_indicators: "Development of Climate Change Adaptation Plans"; "Setting up forecasting, warning and response systems"; "climate prediction models for extreme phenomena"; "population warning"
co_benefits: Disaster risk reduction for non-climate hazards; improved public safety/emergency management capacity; potential health system preparedness benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Early warning system “setting up” could also be coded as infrastructure-investment (monitoring networks/data platforms), but the statement is framed at a program/plan establishment level without explicit capital investment details; classified as planning-strategy.
